title: The Mountain Wreath
description: A canonical South Slavic poem tied to nationhood, faith, and controversial violence.
descriptionParagraphs: A canonical South Slavic poem tied to nationhood, faith, and controversial violence.

The Mountain Wreath approaches nationalism, religion, and identity politics through voice, rhythm, and compression rather than plot alone. Its poetic form lets feeling, argument, and public speech overlap in a way prose often cannot.

Its staying power comes from the fit between subject and form. The language itself becomes part of the argument, so the work matters not just for what it says about nationalism, religion, and identity politics but for how it sounds and moves on the page.

overview.englishDescription: A canonical South Slavic poem tied to nationhood, faith, and controversial violence.
banningParagraphs: The Mountain Wreath entered censorship debates as a epic poem associated with nationalism, religion, and identity politics. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around ethnic conflict and hate speech.

The earliest event currently captured here is late 1990s in Bosnia and Herzegovina, where School authorities under international administration removed from schools. Officials objected to how the text could inflame postwar sectarian politics. The case turns on the difference between literary canon and contemporary conflict management.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Wretched of the Earth
counterReadings.author: Frantz Fanon
counterReadings.note: A strong counter-reading for colonial rule, racial hierarchy, and imperial cultural power.

counterReadings.title: Everybody's Protest Novel
counterReadings.author: James Baldwin
counterReadings.note: Sharp criticism of sentimental protest fiction and its political limits.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
